name: Rangda
description: Rangda is a demon queen figure in Balinese mythology. Terrifying in appearance, she is the leader of the leyaks, evil spirits that haunt cemeteries and feed on human flesh. Rangda embodies darkness and destruction, representing the antithesis of the benevolent Barong. She is often depicted as an old woman with long, unkempt hair, pendulous breasts, and sharp claws. Her face is a grotesque mask with bulging eyes, a long tongue, and protruding fangs. Rangda's power stems from her mastery of black magic, which she uses to inflict disease, death, and chaos upon the world. Despite her malevolent nature, Rangda is also a powerful protector of Bali, maintaining the balance between good and evil. The ritual combat between Rangda and Barong symbolizes this eternal struggle, ensuring harmony within the Balinese cosmos. Her image is ubiquitous in Balinese art and performance, reminding people of the constant presence of both positive and negative forces in their lives. She is a complex figure, both feared and revered, embodying the duality inherent in Balinese cosmology. She is a widow, cursed and transformed into a demon after being wronged. The dance between Rangda and Barong is a crucial part of Balinese culture.
subtitle: Demon Queen of Balinese Mythology
appearance: Rangda is depicted as an old woman with long, unkempt hair, pendulous breasts, sharp claws, and a fearsome mask. Her face features bulging eyes, a long, protruding tongue, and sharp fangs. She often wears a white cloth and is adorned with jewelry and garlands of flowers, contrasting with her terrifying appearance. Her skin is often depicted as wrinkled and pale.
habitat: Cemeteries, temples, and anywhere dark magic is practiced in Bali.
behavior: Rangda is malevolent and destructive, seeking to spread disease, death, and chaos. She commands the leyaks and uses her black magic to harm humans. Despite her evil nature, she is also a powerful force for balance, engaging in a perpetual struggle with Barong to maintain cosmic harmony. She is easily angered and vengeful.

keyMyths.title: The Widow Calon Arang
keyMyths.description: Rangda is often associated with the historical figure of Calon Arang, a widow accused of practicing black magic. Legend says she was cursed after being denied a suitable husband for her daughter, transforming her into the demon queen Rangda.

keyMyths.title: The Battle with Barong
keyMyths.description: The eternal battle between Rangda and Barong represents the struggle between good and evil. Barong, a benevolent spirit, attempts to defeat Rangda and restore balance, but the fight is never truly won, reflecting the ongoing nature of this cosmic conflict.

keyMyths.title: Rangda's Power over Leyaks
keyMyths.description: Rangda commands an army of leyaks, evil spirits that transform into animals and other monstrous forms. These leyaks carry out Rangda's bidding, spreading disease and terror throughout Bali.

sources.author: Miguel Covarrubias
sources.description: Classic ethnographic study of Balinese culture, including descriptions of Rangda and her significance.
sources.text: Classic ethnographic study of Balinese culture, including descriptions of Rangda and her significance.

sources.author: Adrian Vickers
sources.description: Explores the historical and cultural context of Rangda within Balinese society.
sources.text: Explores the historical and cultural context of Rangda within Balinese society.

sources.author: Fred B. Eiseman
sources.description: Provides insight into the visible (sekala) and invisible (niskala) aspects of Balinese culture, including Rangda's role in the spiritual realm.
